cards.front: What is an iterator in C++?
cards.back: An object that can traverse (iterate through) a container, providing access to elements one at a time. Think of it as a pointer-like object.

cards.text: To get an iterator to the beginning of a container, use {{c1::std::begin(container)}}. To get one past the end, use {{c2::std::end(container)}}.
Iterators support pointer-like operations: {{c1::operator*}} to dereference, {{c2::operator++}} to advance, and {{c3::operator!=}} to compare.

cards.question: What does this iterator loop do?
cards.answer: Prints each element of the array separated by spaces.
cards.explanation: It starts at begin(), advances until end(), dereferencing the iterator (*it) to access each element.

cards.front: What is the relationship between iterators and pointers?
cards.back: Pointers are a simple form of iterator. For basic arrays, std::begin() and std::end() return actual pointers. Iterators generalize this concept for all container types.

cards.text: std::begin() and std::end() work with {{c1::all}} container types (arrays, std::vector, std::list, etc.), providing a {{c2::consistent}} interface.

cards.concept: Why use iterators instead of indices
cards.explanation:
Iterators provide:
  • Universal interface for all container types
  • Work with containers that don't support indexing (e.g., std::list)
  • Required by standard library algorithms
  • More flexible than raw array access

cards.front: Why does std::end() return an iterator one past the last element instead of to the last element?
cards.back: This allows the half-open range [begin, end) to work consistently, even for empty containers, and simplifies loop conditions (it != end()).

cards.text: When declaring an iterator, use {{c1::auto}} to avoid writing complex iterator type names like std::vector<int>::iterator.

cards.question: What's wrong with this iterator usage?
cards.answer: Missing dereference operator (*). Should be: std::cout << *it;
cards.explanation: Iterators must be dereferenced with * to access the value they point to, just like pointers.

cards.back: A range [begin, end) that includes the begin element but excludes the end element. Used by iterators.

cards.front: What is the relationship between range-based for loops and iterators?
cards.back: Range-based for loops use iterators internally. They're syntactic sugar that automatically calls std::begin() and std::end().
